summary: Jeff Atwood examines a Cornell psychology paper on the Dunning-Kruger effect, which shows that incompetent people lack the metacognitive skills to recognize their own incompetence. The research found that people scoring in the bottom quartile vastly overestimated their abilities, placing themselves in the 62nd percentile when they were actually in the 12th. Atwood connects this to software development, noting parallels with wicked problems, iterative development, and extreme skill disparities in programming. He argues that mocking bad code on sites like The Daily WTF is amusing but ultimately unhelpful, since incompetent developers remain unaware of their deficiencies. The real challenge is bootstrapping developers' skills to a level where they can recognize and correct their own mistakes.
key_points: The same skills needed to produce competent work are the same skills needed to recognize competent work—lacking one means lacking both
Research shows people in the bottom quartile dramatically overestimate their abilities, thinking they're above average
This paradox manifests in software development through wicked problems, iterative development needs, and extreme skill disparities
Improving someone's skills also improves their ability to recognize their previous limitations
Mocking incompetent code is entertaining but doesn't solve the underlying problem of skill deficiency
money_quotes: The skills that enable one to construct a grammatical sentence are the same skills necessary to recognize a grammatical sentence, and thus are the same skills necessary to determine if a grammatical mistake has been made.
To lack the former is to be deficient in the latter.
Participants scoring in the bottom quartile on tests of humor, grammar, and logic grossly overestimated their test performance and ability.
Although their test scores put them in the 12th percentile, they estimated themselves to be in the 62nd.
Paradoxically, improving the skills of participants, and thus increasing their metacognitive competence, helped them recognize the limitations of their abilities.
The worst software developers are profoundly bad; the best software developers are absurdly good.
Making fun of incompetence is amusing, but until these developers' skills are bootstrapped to a moderate level, they're going to keep pounding out more and more WTFs.
key_insight: Incompetent developers can't recognize their own incompetence because the skills required to write good code are the same skills required to evaluate code quality—making education, not ridicule, the only path forward.
